name: Crocus Sativus
summary: Lean bulk ingestion row for Crocus sativus.
description: Lean bulk ingestion row for Crocus sativus. Key active compounds include Crocin; Crocetin.
contraindications: SSRI or SNRI or MAOI antidepressant medication due to serotonergic activity that may have an additive effect and theoretically raise serotonin syndrome risk
antihypertensive medication such as calcium channel blockers or ACE inhibitors or beta-blockers because saffron independently lowers blood pressure and may cause additive hypotension
pregnancy without clinician guidance because doses above culinary amounts have documented uterine-stimulant and abortifacient effects
known allergy to saffron or the Iridaceae plant family

sources.title: The Effects of Crocus sativus (Saffron) on ADHD: A Systematic Review
The Efficacy of Saffron in the Treatment of Mild to Moderate Depression: A Meta-analysis
Saffron (Crocus sativus L.): A Source of Nutrients for Health and for the Treatment of Neuropsychiatric and Age-Related Diseases

New horizons for the study of saffron (Crocus sativus L.) and its active ingredients in the management of neurological and psychiatric disorders: A systematic review of clinical evidence and mechanisms
sources.authors: Shufen Han; Yifei Cao; Xingrong Wu; Jiaoyang Xu; Zizheng Nie; Yue Qiu
sources.note: saffron depression/anxiety/sleep/cognition/ADHD broad evidence and safety/tolerability

claimMap.claim: Saffron shows promising multi-domain evidence for mood, anxiety, sleep, cognition, ADHD/OCD contexts, with few side effects in trials.
claimMap.notes: Saffron shows promising multi-domain evidence for mood, anxiety, sleep, cognition, ADHD/OCD contexts, with few side effects in trials.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: 4 to 48 weeks; saffron or saffron extracts

dosage: Common clinical saffron studies often use standardized extract ranges such as 30 mg/day, but Pass 5 source should be used to verify dose per target condition before importing dose. | Saffron stigma/extracts and active ingredients; standardization matters.
typical_dosage: Common clinical saffron studies often use standardized extract ranges such as 30 mg/day, but Pass 5 source should be used to verify dose per target condition before importing dose. | Saffron stigma/extracts and active ingredients; standardization matters.
